In agent-memory research, two benchmarks are virtually inescapable — everyone runs them: LoCoMo and LongMemEval. But does a high score on either truly prove anything? Are there more targeted benchmarks better suited to specific use cases? Should memory evaluation be reduced to accuracy alone? What other metrics matter, and how are the data synthesis pipelines behind these benchmarks actually constructed?
These are the questions this article sets out to answer.
Overall Agent-memory benchmarks broadly fall into two categories: conversational memory and agent task improvement. The former concerns whether the system can remember important user information; the latter focuses on whether the agent can reduce similar types of errors over time.
Naturally, the evaluation approaches differ — for task improvement, standard coding benchmarks can often serve the purpose. This article focuses on the former: conversational memory.
Memory benchmarks can be understood along two orthogonal axes.
Axis 1: What content / scenario is being tested (horizontal) — factual conversational memory (LoCoMo / LongMemEval), personalization & user profiling (PersonaMem / PersonaLens), experiential / environmental memory (LongMemEval-V2), and multimodal memory.
Axis 2: What capability dimension is being tested (vertical) — lifecycle abilities (update, conflict, forgetting, abstention, temporal reasoning). These cut across all scenarios.
Long-term Conversational Memory The earliest and most frequently evaluated category of personal memory is long-term conversational memory: given dialogues spanning multiple turns and multiple sessions, can the agent remember user-relevant information and correctly recall it in subsequent questions or responses?
This type of benchmark closely resembles traditional long-context QA. The emphasis is on whether the agent can locate, understand, and apply user-related memories across very long conversation histories — both within long sessions and across sessions.
LoCoMo 📄 LoCoMo Paper · GitHub Repository
LoCoMo (Long Conversation Memory) is one of the most frequently cited benchmarks in agent-memory research.
LoCoMo’s data is generated through a hybrid human-machine pipeline: starting from predefined personas and temporal event graphs, two LLM agents generate long conversation skeletons, which human annotators then verify and edit to ensure long-range consistency, event grounding, and multimodal content plausibility. The public release contains 10 ultra-long conversations, each averaging roughly 300 turns and 9K tokens, spanning up to 35 sessions.
LoCoMo defines three evaluation tasks.
The first task is Question Answering. This is the part most commonly used by subsequent memory system papers. QA questions fall into five categories:
Category What It Tests Challenge Count Single-hop Factual recall within a single session Finding answers directly from one part of the dialogue 841 Multi-hop Synthesizing information across multiple sessions Piecing together evidence from multiple sources 282 Temporal reasoning Temporal reasoning Understanding “before,” “after,” “months later,” and other temporal relations 321 Open-domain / commonsense Dialogue information + external commonsense knowledge Not pure retrieval — requires commonsense inference 96 Adversarial Unanswerable or trick questions Preventing the model from hallucinating in long contexts 446 The second task is Event Summarization. The model must summarize the conversation history into a temporal event graph, testing whether it understands events, causal relationships, and temporal ordering in long-term dialogues.
The third task is Multimodal Dialogue Generation. LoCoMo’s dialogues include image sharing and image reactions, so this task evaluates whether the model can combine past dialogue and multimodal cues to generate coherent responses consistent with character state and long-term narrative.
From a data structure perspective (locomo10.json), each element in the JSON array represents one complete long conversation with its annotations.
Evaluation methodology and metrics. Evaluation is not LLM-Judge-dominated.
QA: normalized / partial-match F1; RAG systems additionally report retrieval recall / recall@k. Event Summarization: ROUGE + FactScore, where FactScore decomposes into atomic facts and computes precision / recall / F1. Multimodal Dialogue Generation: BLEU-1/2, ROUGE-L, MM-Relevance. Its limitations, however, are clear. At roughly 9K tokens on average, the public version is no longer sufficient in 2026 to meaningfully differentiate long-context models. Furthermore, most follow-up work focuses primarily on LoCoMo’s QA component, reducing it to a text retrieval / QA benchmark; the event summarization and multimodal dialogue generation tasks remain underutilized in practical system comparisons.
This has led to targeted corrections such as LoCoMo-Plus and LoCoMo-Refined. LoCoMo-Plus’s direction is particularly noteworthy: it attempts to move from surface-level factual recall to cognitive memory — testing whether a model can apply implicit user states, goals, values, and constraints from earlier dialogues in subsequent scenarios.
📄 LoCoMo-Plus Paper · LoCoMo-Refined GitHub Repository
LongMemEval Compared to LoCoMo, LongMemEval is more directly oriented toward long-term interaction memory for chat assistants. Its goal is to evaluate whether an assistant can record, retrieve, update, and reason about user information across sustained, multi-session user interactions.
📄 LongMemEval Paper · GitHub Repository
LongMemEval contains 500 questions covering five categories of long-term memory capability:
Capability Meaning Information Extraction Recalling specific details from long interaction histories, including details once mentioned by the user or assistant Multi-Session Reasoning Synthesizing information across multiple sessions for aggregation, comparison, or inference Knowledge Update Recognizing changes in user information and updating old memories with new information Temporal Reasoning Understanding temporal attributes in user information, including explicit timestamps and session timestamps Abstention Knowing when to refuse to answer rather than hallucinate, when the relevant information is absent from history LongMemEval offers different difficulty tiers. LongMemEval-S provides roughly 115K tokens of history per question, typically spanning 30–40 sessions; LongMemEval-M scales to roughly 500 sessions and 1.5M tokens. This makes it far more suitable than LoCoMo for testing the memory scalability of real personal assistants over extended use.
For evaluation, LongMemEval employs LLM-as-a-Judge, with GPT-4o judging whether a model’s answer is correct. It also proposes a useful memory system analysis framework: decomposing a long-term memory system into three stages — indexing, retrieval, and reading.
From a personal memory perspective, LongMemEval is closer to real user-assistant scenarios than LoCoMo. It tests not only “does it remember,” but also whether user information updates over time, whether information can be synthesized across sessions, whether temporal reasoning is handled correctly, and whether the system refuses to answer when information is absent.
That said, LongMemEval remains fundamentally a long-context QA / retrieval-oriented benchmark. Its core evaluation format still revolves around finding evidence in chat histories and answering questions. But real-world agents face a harder problem: state is dynamic, making the benchmark susceptible to retrieval engineering.
From LongMemEval to LongMemEval-V2 📄 LongMemEval-V2 GitHub Repository
No longer merely testing “can a chat assistant remember long-term conversations,” but rather “can an agent form long-term experiential memory in complex environments.”
V2’s object of study is no longer ordinary user-assistant chat history, but web-agent trajectories, corresponding to five categories of memory ability:
V2 Capability Meaning Static state recall Remembering static environmental information, such as page layouts, module functions, important locations Dynamic state tracking Tracking how states and actions change the environment over time Workflow knowledge Remembering the procedures for completing recurring tasks in specific environments Environment gotchas Recognizing recurring local failure patterns and environmental traps Premise awareness Knowing which assumptions valid in other environments do not hold in the current deployment V2’s context scale explodes:
small tier: 25M tokens medium tier: 115M tokens This far exceeds mainstream context windows.
This represents arguably one of the biggest directional shifts in AI memory research over the past year.
“How can LLMs remember long-term conversations” → “How can agents accumulate long-term work experience”
Or put differently: chatbot memory → agentic / experiential memory.
From a data synthesis pipeline perspective:
LongMemEval is constructed by first designing the user memories to be tested, then embedding those memories into long chat histories. It roughly follows three steps:
question curation: An ontology of 164 user attributes (e.g., lifestyle, belongings) is defined. An LLM generates user background paragraphs for each attribute, from which candidate QA pairs are generated (with human filtering, rewriting, and difficulty calibration). Annotators decompose answers into one or more evidence statements (some carrying temporal information). evidence session construction: Each evidence statement is embedded into a task-oriented user-assistant session, using self-chat to generate dialogue. The user does not explicitly state the evidence; instead, information is revealed indirectly through requests for help, advice, or task completion. history compilation: Relevant evidence sessions are inserted into a longer user-assistant chat history. Irrelevant sessions come from two sources: self-chat sessions synthesized from other non-conflicting user attributes, and ShareGPT + UltraChat. All sessions are assigned reasonable timestamps, producing a coherent, length-configurable long-term chat history. At its core, this remains needle-in-a-haystack for personal memory.
By contrast, LongMemEval-V2 is constructed by first collecting agent trajectories from task execution in environments, then extracting from those trajectories the knowledge an experienced agent ought to remember.
It roughly follows four steps:
trajectory collection: Web-agent trajectories are collected from WebArena, WorkArena, and WorkArena++ via the AgentLab unified interface. Each trajectory includes task goal, state observations, screenshots, accessibility tree, actions, and state transitions. Both successful and failed trajectories are collected through rejection sampling. question annotation: Questions are constructed around five memory ability categories: static state recall, dynamic state tracking, workflow knowledge, environment gotchas, and premise awareness. The model is tested on whether it can answer candidate questions without trajectory evidence — if it can, the questions are filtered or rewritten (preventing the model from answering via public knowledge alone). answer trajectory labeling: Each question requires labeling which trajectories genuinely contain answer evidence, with the requirement that evidence be verifiable in screenshots or trajectory states (a batch of seed answer-bearing trajectories is used with Codex to expand the candidate annotation pool, followed by human verification of ambiguous cases). haystack creation: The Small tier constructs one shared 100-trajectory haystack each for WebArena and ServiceNow; the Medium tier constructs roughly 500-trajectory question-specific haystacks for each question. EverMemBench 📄 EverMemBench Paper
From dyadic conversations to multi-person collaboration. Can an agent embedded in long-term team collaboration maintain memory across multiple people, groups, and timelines?
EverMemBench simulates enterprise collaboration scenarios. The data includes multiple projects, sub-projects, employees, group conversations, and evidence-grounded QA. The overall structure resembles:
project ├── sub-project / group chat │ ├── day 1 messages │ ├── day 2 messages │ └── ... ├── employees / personas ├── task timeline └── QA items with evidence references Evaluation pipeline:
Add → Search → Answer → Evaluate EverMemBench’s QA is organized across three dimensions and nine subtasks:
Dimension Subtasks Count What It Evaluates Fine-grained Recall Single-hop Retrieval, Multi-hop Trajectory, Temporal Duration 762 Whether precise facts, links, dates, people, task statuses can be found Memory Awareness Constraint, Proactivity, Update 1,097 Whether past rules, constraints, and updates can be applied to new scenarios Profile Understanding Style, Skill, Role 541 Whether long-term interaction enables understanding of personal style, skills, and roles EverMemBench’s evaluation approach can be divided into two layers: task protocol and scoring method.
For task protocol, it uses a streaming multi-group protocol: during the ingestion phase, the system receives daily batches in chronological order and must maintain its own memory state; during the evaluation phase, questions are posed. This protocol tests how the system writes, updates, retrieves, and uses memory while continuously receiving information, rather than dumping all history into context at once.
For scoring:
Question Type Evaluation Method Multiple-choice Directly determine whether A/B/C/D is correct Open-ended LLM-as-a-Judge determines whether the generated answer and gold answer are semantically equivalent Memory system Add → Search → Answer → Evaluate Full-context LLM Feed the complete dialogue as context directly, without retrieval Data construction can be summarized as:
blueprint and profile generation → chunk-wise dialogue synthesis → evidence-grounded QA construction User Profiling and Personalization PersonaMem 📄 PersonaMem Paper · GitHub Repository
The core question is not “can it recite a particular fact,” but: can the model form and update user profiles across long-range, multi-turn, multi-session user interaction histories, and use the most current user state to generate personalized responses?
Three capabilities: whether the model can internalize users’ intrinsic characteristics and preferences; whether it can track the temporal evolution of user profiles / preferences; and whether it can generate personalized responses in new scenarios accordingly.
The setup: given the user-LLM interaction history up to a certain point in time, plus a first-person in-situ user query, the model must select the response that best matches the current user state.
It tests four things:
Remembering facts / preferences users have shared: e.g., a user once expressed a preference for a certain type of music, diet, or learning style.
Updating memories: Preferences change — the model must not cling to old preferences, e.g., shifting from liking pizza to going gluten-free due to a gluten allergy.
Retrieving relevant fragments from history: Information may appear far back in long histories, with “lost in the middle” problems.
Applying memories to responses, not merely parroting: The hardest part is giving new recommendations based on current preferences, or transferring to new scenarios. V1 found models perform reasonably on simple factual recall, but are significantly weaker at “giving new suggestions” and “applying user preferences across scenarios.”
V1 data scale: roughly 20 personas, 180+ interaction histories, each history up to 60 multi-turn sessions, covering 15 personalized task scenarios; the HF dataset provides 32K, 128K, and 1M context-length versions.
It defines 7 types of in-situ user queries to cover different memory / personalization skills:
Type What It Tests Recall user-shared facts Remembering facts, activities, and interests the user has shared Suggest new ideas Recommending new things not present in history, while respecting user constraints Acknowledge latest user preferences Recognizing the user’s most recent preferences, not outdated ones Track full preference evolution Tracking the complete change in preferences from past to present Revisit reasons behind preference updates Remembering the reasons behind preference changes Provide preference-aligned recommendations Proactively offering suggestions consistent with current preferences Generalize to new scenarios Transferring user characteristics learned from other scenarios to new tasks V1’s primary evaluation is 4-way multiple-choice accuracy: the model sees the full conversation history, the current user query, and four candidate responses, and selects the response that best matches the current user profile.
V2 improvements:
V1 Issue / Limitation V2 Corresponding Change Small scale: V1 has only ~20 personas, 180+ histories, 15 task scenarios V2 scales to 1,000 personas, 26,100 preferences / Q&A pairs, 325 conversation topics, 335 user query topics. Preferences relatively explicit: V2 paper notes V1 still focused mainly on explicit preferences V2 focuses on implicit personas / implicit preferences, embedding preferences within realistic interactions like email rewriting, translation, knowledge queries, and photo sharing. Evaluation biased toward multiple-choice: V1’s primary evaluation is MCQ; generative evaluation was constrained by logit availability V2 provides both MCQ and open-ended evaluation; open-ended uses LLM-as-a-judge; the paper reports aggregating judgments from three GPT-5-Chat judges. No train/validation split for model improvement V2 is not just a benchmark — it provides train/val/test splits: text benchmark 5,000, train 18,500, val 2,600; multimodal benchmark 5,000, train 19,200, val 2,110. Insufficient privacy/control scenarios for real-world use V2 adds scenarios where the user leaks sensitive information or explicitly asks “don’t remember this preference,” and requires target answers to avoid using sensitive/private information. More like a long-context test, high cost V2 proposes/evaluates agentic memory: compressing long histories into roughly 2K-token, readable, updatable memory; the paper claims roughly 16× token savings relative to 32K context, and surpasses direct long-context models on both MCQ and open-ended. User preferences easily confused with others’ information V2 data columns include an explicit who label, distinguishing whether the current preference belongs to the user or someone else, and adds unrelated math/code questions as distractors. Single modality / limited scenario coverage V2 has both text and multimodal benchmark suites, and includes multimodal photo sharing, multilingual translation, health/medical, therapy, and other scenarios. V2’s positioning reflects a clear pivot: from “explicit/dynamic preference memory” to “implicit preference inference + trainable agentic memory.”
V2 particularly focuses on cases where user preferences are not stated directly but are embedded in long dialogues — for instance, when a user asks the model to polish an email, the email content may inadvertently reveal seasonal allergies.
Each benchmark row in V2 includes: persona_id, path to the full persona file, links to 32K/128K chat history, user_query, correct_answer, three incorrect_answers, current preference, preference category, relevant dialogue snippets, whether the preference belongs to the user, whether it has been updated, old preference, sensitive information, and token distance from relevant snippet to query.
🤗 PersonaMem-v2 Dataset
V2’s primary evaluation metrics:
MCQ accuracy: The model selects the most personalized answer from four plausible options. Open-ended personalization accuracy / judge score: The model generates a free-form response, then LLM-as-a-judge determines whether it reflects the ground-truth preference; the paper reports aggregation from multiple GPT-5-Chat judges. Grouped analysis: Performance is broken down by preference type, scenario, whether the preference was dynamically updated, who, distance to relevant snippet, and other dimensions. The official evaluation scripts also generate these category breakdowns. PersonaLens Where PersonaMem treats personalization as a memory QA problem, PersonaLens is more concerned with whether personalization gets squeezed out by task objectives during task completion.
📄 PersonaLens Paper
In terms of composition, it has 1,500 user profiles (demographic attributes drawn from the PRISM Alignment dataset, with preferences generated by an LLM based on demographics to ensure internal consistency), covering roughly 20 domains from Books, Finance, Flights to Restaurants.
Evaluation methodology: dual-agent automatic evaluation — a user agent (implemented with Claude 3 Sonnet) simulates a user with a specific profile and context conversing with the assistant under test; a judge agent uses LLM-as-a-Judge to rate personalization, response quality, and task completion, distinguishing between single-domain (TSD) and multi-domain (TMD) tasks.
A key finding: there exists a trade-off between task completion rate (TCR) and personalization — the more the assistant buries its head in getting the task done, the more likely it is to ignore user preferences.
By contrast, static QA has no “task objective” competing with personalization for attention.
Multimodal Personal Memory Memory originates as text. But real personal memory is clearly more than that.
Can an agent build long-term memory across text, images, and video, and then invoke those memories in downstream tasks?
In much of what is called multimodal memory, images are merely captioned into text and fed back into the text pipeline. LoCoMo itself includes a multimodal component (image sharing), but the vast majority of subsequent work has reduced it to pure text QA.
A useful way to think about this is to ask: at which stage does multimodality actually play a role?
Input / storage side: preserving visual information across sessions.
A user shares an image in an earlier session; the system must remember its content later.
📄 Mem-Gallery Paper
Mem-Gallery explicitly points out that existing benchmarks either evaluate memory in purely textual multi-session settings, or evaluate multimodal understanding in localized single-session contexts — none genuinely assess how multimodal memory is preserved, organized, and evolved over long dialogue trajectories. It evaluates along three dimensions: memory extraction & test-time adaptation, memory reasoning, and memory knowledge management.
Retrieval side: cross-modal retrieval within visual history. Storage is only the first step; whether the right image / frame can be found based on the current need is another matter entirely.
Layered with personalization: multimodal × user modeling.
Persona-MME targets long-term personalized multimodal LLMs by constructing persistent multimodal persona contexts (user profile, frequently occurring social relationships, objects the user owns) and then generating three types of multimodal dialogue — explicit entities, implicit facts, and distractors — to control the memory signals introduced.
Layered with real / embodied scenarios. This falls within the embodied / world model direction: egocentric wearable assistants, robot memory, video world model memory capabilities, etc. (Beyond the scope of this article.)
On the positive side, Mem-Gallery, MemLens, and similar work from early 2026 represent a correction to the “images get captioned away” problem.
But this category as a whole is still very young. It has not yet converged on a universally-adopted benchmark like LoCoMo; modality coverage, task formats, and evaluation standards remain inconsistent across efforts.
Memory Lifecycle Capabilities The preceding sections were organized by “what content is being tested”: conversational facts, user profiles, multimodal.
But can the agent update old memories? Handle conflicting information? Know when to forget or abstain? Track how preferences change over time? These are the lifecycle capabilities of memory.
The lifecycle landscape is fragmented — a collection of specialized benchmarks each targeting a single capability, most emerging only from late 2025 through 2026. This is itself a signal: the field is only just realizing that accuracy alone is not enough. Specialized evaluations are beginning to appear but have not yet converged.
Below, organized by capability dimension, with representative benchmarks and the metrics — distinct from accuracy — that each introduces.
Update
A memory system must remember the most recent version, not the first thing that was said.
LongMemEval already includes a dedicated knowledge-update subset (described earlier). Going further, some work has turned this into a metric rather than just a data split: Forgetting-Aware Memory Accuracy specifically penalizes reliance on stale or invalidated memories.
Conflict Resolution
When two contradictory pieces of information coexist in memory, can the system ensure only the latest, correct one takes effect?
MemoryAgentBench addresses this: decomposing memory capability into accurate retrieval, test-time learning, long-range comprehension, and conflict resolution, with a dedicated Conflict Resolution Accuracy metric ensuring that when contradictions exist, only the most recent valid fact is used.
Forgetting / Selective Retention
Not all information should be kept forever: expired information and anything the user explicitly asks “don’t remember this” should be discarded.
MemoryAgentBench uses its newly constructed FactConsolidation dataset to specifically test selective forgetting.
User-initiated forgetting is also tied to privacy, as in PersonaMem-v2’s privacy scenarios (user requests not to remember a certain preference).
Abstention
If you don’t know, say you don’t know. A memory system must recognize “insufficient evidence” and abstain, rather than hallucinate.
LongMemEval specifically includes abstention-type questions to test this, with corresponding metrics of abstention rate / abstention accuracy — again, not captured by standard accuracy.
From Passive Recall to Memory-Driven Action
MemoryArena shows that models scoring near-perfect on recall benchmarks plummet in performance the moment memory must drive action. Many so-called memory benchmarks are essentially retrieval benchmarks, testing “can the needle be found” rather than “can memory actually be put to use.”
Evaluation Metrics: Beyond Accuracy If we only look at accuracy, what gets missed?
Borrowing an existing layered framework, a recent memory survey proposed a four-layer evaluation stack that serves as a useful skeleton for this section.
Layer 1: Task Effectiveness
Did the task actually get done?
Common metrics include success rate, factual correctness, plan completion rate. In the QA format typical of conversational memory benchmarks, these are the familiar: token-level F1, BLEU, binary/rating judgments from LLM-as-a-Judge, and MCQ accuracy.
These metrics are not equivalent to one another — which one you choose changes the conclusion. Even though LLM-as-a-Judge is closer to semantic correctness, it introduces the judge’s own biases.
At this point, “who is SOTA” depends on which metric you trust.
Layer 2: Memory Quality
Key metrics include: precision / recall of retrieved records, contradiction rate, staleness distribution, and coverage of task-relevant facts.
Some work on LongMemEval-S has achieved nearly 99% retrieval recall for certain question types using retrieval alone — but that’s retrieval recall, not end-to-end accuracy.
Staleness distribution and contradiction rate focus on “how much of the stored memory has expired or is internally contradictory.”
Correspondingly, specialized metrics have emerged, such as Forgetting-Aware Memory Accuracy (penalizing reliance on stale memories) and conflict resolution accuracy (ensuring only the latest version of contradictory information takes effect).
Layer 3: Efficiency
Core metrics: latency per memory operation, prompt tokens consumed by memory content, retrieval call count per step, and storage growth over time.
A methodological detail worth noting: rigorous work (e.g., HiMem) reports latency by measuring only the time spent on memory retrieval itself, excluding LLM inference, to isolate memory system efficiency and avoid contamination by the speed of the underlying model. When reading efficiency numbers, pay attention to which segment is actually being measured.
Layer 4: Governance
Does the memory system comply with privacy and control requirements?
Metrics include: privacy leakage rate, deletion compliance (when the user says “don’t remember / delete this,” does the system actually comply), and access-scope violations (unauthorized access, especially in multi-user shared memory scenarios).
PersonaMem-v2 already incorporates such scenarios: users leaking sensitive information, or explicitly requesting “don’t remember this preference,” with target answers required to avoid using such sensitive / private information.
These four layers are often in tension with one another.
A thorough system evaluation should therefore present an operating curve across the four layers (a tunable trade-off among accuracy / store-size / latency), rather than a single isolated best score.
Data Synthesis Methods Before discussing any memory benchmark, a more fundamental question should be asked: how were these multi-turn dialogues constructed? Because the synthesis method almost entirely determines what a benchmark actually tests — and what it is inherently incapable of testing.
Current mainstream benchmark dialogue sources fall roughly into three tiers:
Purely human-authored real data (virtually nonexistent) LLM-generated skeleton + human verification (exemplified by LoCoMo: based on predefined personas and temporal event graphs, dialogue skeletons are generated using an LLM agent architecture, then human annotators verify and edit for long-range consistency) Needle-in-a-haystack assembly (exemplified by LongMemEval: questions written by human experts, evidence-bearing sessions simulated by LLMs and manually edited, while the vast majority of irrelevant sessions are sampled from ShareGPT, UltraChat, and simulated dialogues) Personalization-oriented benchmarks rely more heavily on persona-driven fully-automated synthesis. PersonaMem uses a GPT-4o-driven modular pipeline to generate multi-session, temporally-structured interaction histories from personas, embedding preferences that evolve over time within the dialogues; its in-situ query appends the user’s current first-person question to the end of the history, testing whether “the model can answer using the most recent preferences.”
Since needle-in-a-haystack is fundamentally a needle-finding test, what it predominantly measures is retrieval capability within long contexts, rather than the organizational and evolutionary capabilities of a memory system.
When LongMemEval selects haystack sessions, it deliberately chooses sessions similar in topic and format to the evidence sessions, but without introducing conflicting information, so as not to invalidate the questions. This engineering-sound choice means that this benchmark, from its construction starting point, deliberately avoids knowledge conflicts — and is therefore inherently incapable of testing conflict resolution.
As for the common concern about “whether LLM-generated data is trustworthy”: the answer lies not in LLM hallucination per se, but in the filtering and verification after generation. PersonaMem-v2, for example, applies four hard filters to every Q&A pair — the question must be unanswerable without the history (preventing data leakage and generation artifacts), the correct option must genuinely reflect user preferences, incorrect options must never reflect them, and the format must not retain generation traces. Ultimately, only roughly 30% of generated Q&A pairs pass all filters.
The pass rate itself reveals that, without rigorous filtering, a significant proportion of synthetic dialogues contain shortcuts exploitable by models.
Thus, the synthesis pipeline itself defines the boundaries of what a benchmark can test.
Closing No single benchmark can cover everything that agent-memory entails. Every facet — factual recall, personalized inference, multimodality, update, conflict, forgetting, abstention, memory-driven action — requires purpose-built data and purpose-designed metrics.
So when an agent-memory project claims yet another SOTA, the set of questions worth asking remains the same: SOTA on which benchmark? What core capability does that benchmark measure, and what properties does it miss? What does its data construction allow it to test, and what is it structurally unable to test? Which layers of metrics does it report, and are the missing layers precisely the ones it sacrificed?
summary: A comprehensive survey of agent-memory benchmarks: from LoCoMo and LongMemEval to PersonaMem, covering evaluation methodology, metrics, and data synthesis pipelines across conversational memory, user profiling, and multimodal memory.
title: Beyond SOTA: What Agent-Memory Benchmarks Actually Measure — and What They Miss

content:
Prologue: From an Old Question to a New One I remember when I first started out in this industry, I followed a question on Zhihu:
“The internet has been fought to a bloody red ocean, yet smart manufacturing remains a wasteland — why won’t BAT (Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent) step into it?”
Over five years later, this question still has no definitive answer — but the times have changed.
Back then, the subtext was: technology is scarce, industries are waiting to be transformed, and as long as the top-tier players are willing to enter the fray, things will happen. Reality has since falsified that assumption in nearly every way imaginable. And now, LLMs have pushed the “technology is scarce” premise even further. But ask anyone pushing AI adoption in a traditional industry, and odds are they’ll tell you: it’s not that simple.
And so this old question takes on a new form in the AI era:
“AI is already this powerful — why can’t these industries get moving?”
Recently, the term FDE (Forward Deployed Engineer) has suddenly caught fire among major AI companies — and at its core, it’s a response to this very question: if the models are already powerful enough, then the “last mile” bottleneck must lie in the customer site, in the data, in the process, in the organization.
This article is my attempt to answer this old question, drawing on years of observation and hands-on practice.
Let me put the conclusion up front:
What most industries are missing right now isn’t AI — it’s X-Ops.
In other words, the question has never been “should we adopt AI?” — it’s whether this industry, this company, this production line, this workflow has been transformed into a system that AI can plug into, understand, participate in, and feed back into.
Why Has the Software Industry Been Hit Hardest by AI? It’s not AI. It’s DevOps.
If you look at the fields where AI has truly “broken in” over the past two years, the software industry itself appears to have taken the biggest hit. The reason the software industry has been hit hardest by AI isn’t that its practitioners are the smartest, nor that models happen to favor code — it’s that over the past two decades, it inadvertently transformed itself into the industry that’s easiest for AI to plug into.
AI-Friendliness Wasn’t Born This Way If I had to list the traits that put AI in its element within the software industry, here’s what I’d pick:
Open corpus, easy to access: GitHub
Short feedback cycles: Write code, compile, run tests, see results — feedback arrives in seconds or minutes
AI-friendly context: Code has comments, modules have interfaces, changes have diffs — nearly everything is textual and structured
Verifiable correctness: Unit tests, integration tests, CI pipelines provide an automatic grading mechanism for whatever the AI writes
These four pieces together set the iteration flywheel in motion: generate → verify → get feedback → fix → regenerate.
But go back to around the year 2000, and the software industry actually looked a lot more like today’s traditional industries. Release cycles were measured in years. A single version would be polished for months and shipped on a CD. What truly changed everything was the transformation of the delivery model brought by the internet. Software no longer went through “manufacture → copy → ship → install” — instead it became “develop → release → get feedback → update → re-release.” Once this closed loop took shape, the entire way the industry organized itself was forced to restructure:
Because you needed to release frequently, you needed version control (Git)
Because you needed to integrate frequently, you needed CI/CD
Because you needed to collaborate across teams, you needed code review, issue tracking, API documentation
Because things could go wrong in production at any moment, you needed logging, monitoring, alerting, observability
Because releasing carried high risk, you needed gradual rollout, rollback, A/B testing
Because collaboration kept scaling up, you needed Agile, DevOps, SRE (Site Reliability Engineering)
This entire apparatus is what we today collectively call DevOps. But the essence of DevOps isn’t a particular combination of tools — it’s an engineering culture that makes the entire process of “how software is produced, delivered, operated, and improved” explicit, versioned, automated, and observable.
The “Byproduct” DevOps Left Behind Became AI’s Food Here’s the most interesting part: DevOps was never designed for AI. It solved problems of human-to-human collaboration, the balance between quality and speed, the engineering challenge of delivering at scale.
But its byproducts almost perfectly hit every piece of infrastructure the AI era demands:
What DevOps left behind What it means for AI Git repos + commit history Traceable evolution history and training data Issues / PRs / Code Review Labeled human decision data Unit tests / CI An automatic grader — AI changes get verified instantly README / API docs / Design Docs Structured context Logs / Traces / Metrics Runtime feedback signals Canary deployment / Rollback A safe trial-and-error mechanism In other words, the software industry spent twenty years turning its production process into a system that is extraordinarily friendly to any external agent — whether that agent is a new hire, an outsourced team, or an AI.
When the coding agent arrived, it didn’t need the software industry to do any extra adapting. It simply plugged into a collaboration infrastructure that had been ready and waiting for it all along.
Knowledge Accumulation Got “Embedded” Into the Production Process This is the single thing the software industry has done that other industries should learn from most.
In many industries, “knowledge management” is something that requires its own project approval, its own budget, its own performance review — someone has to write documentation, maintain the wiki, run training sessions. But because it has no direct link to business output, it’s always the lowest priority, and it never gets done well.
In the software industry, by contrast, knowledge accumulation isn’t an “extra thing” — it’s a byproduct of the production process.
An engineer doesn’t need to make any “extra effort” to do knowledge management. As long as he follows the established practices, knowledge gets automatically captured in a structured form.
Flip the lens to most traditional industries: knowledge lives in people’s heads, in some sheet of some Excel file, in WeChat chat histories. It’s not that knowledge isn’t being produced — it’s that it’s scattered everywhere, exists in all manner of forms, hard to find and hard to read.
The other side of this coin: the reason AI still can’t break into other industries isn’t that the models aren’t strong enough — it’s that those industries haven’t yet prepared themselves to onboard any “external agent” — whether that agent is an AI, or a new employee.
Why Can’t Most Industries Absorb AI? A world without a source of truth.
Dirty, Dirty, Dirty, Dirty Anyone who’s done one or two AI deployment projects in a traditional industry will know these scenes all too well:
Dirty data: numbers don’t match across systems, systems don’t match reality, and nobody knows what a given data field actually means.
Dirty processes: the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) and manuals are written in meticulous detail, but nothing actually runs the way the flowchart says it does.
Dirty systems: a dozen-plus systems spanning different eras and different vendors — interfaces either don’t exist, or they’re SOAP, or the workflow is “export to Excel and import it again.”
Dirty knowledge: the core know-how lives inside a veteran’s head, in WeChat chat logs, in some unmaintained Excel file nobody owns.
Stack all four kinds of dirty on top of each other, and for AI to actually deliver value becomes nearly impossible.
But the deeper problem isn’t that “things are dirty right now” — it’s that over the past decade-plus, digital transformation didn’t actually solve this. In many places, it made it worse.
Most Turned Into Dashboards Over the past decade, nearly every halfway-respectable industrial enterprise has done “digital transformation,” “Industrial Internet,” “smart factory,” “centralized data platform.” But if you look at what these projects actually delivered, the vast majority produced the same thing: a big screen, a few dashboards, a handful of reports.
The various systems, each abbreviated to an English acronym, all sounded right. The project proposals wrote up smoothly. The acceptance demos all ran fine. But they share one common trait: they don’t change how the enterprise operates — they merely “display” the results of how the enterprise already operates.
And so they all ended up as big-screen dashboards for the boss. Display is not the same as change. It became a kind of digital bureaucracy.
Tools, Processes, Organization Let me try to break down the things in an enterprise that “can be digitized / made intelligent” into three layers.
Tool Layer
What this layer does, fundamentally, is: take things where “people already know what to do” and do them faster, more reliably, cheaper.
The vast majority of “AI projects” and “digital transformation projects” operate at this layer. The reason is simple — they’re easy to get approved, easy to sign off, easy to tell a story about. (I also call this peripheral intelligence.)
Process Layer
Take the links that used to depend on human coordination, meeting-driven decisions, and experience-based judgment, and turn them into system-driven chains with feedback and room for optimization.
The value here is far higher than at the Tool Layer, but it demands data that flows, systems that connect, processes that are explicit, and accountability that is clear. This is where the vast majority of enterprises get stuck — because it requires you to clean up the “four kinds of dirty” first.
Organization Layer
The enterprise itself, as a complex system: how it is described, how it is run, how it is improved.
People can handle local problems by experience, but they struggle to manage a high-dimensional, dynamic, multi-objective, tightly-constrained system in real time. What the third layer aims to do is turn this complex system itself into something that can be modeled, monitored, and tuned.
This is the layer where AI-Native should truly come into play. But it has an awkward characteristic: it’s a hard sell.
If you accept this three-layer framework, one thing becomes clear: what makes AI transformation genuinely hard isn’t the models — it’s the dirty work.
And here’s the problem: nobody wanted to do this dirty work over the past few decades, and it’s hard to imagine anyone wanting to do it in the future. Because it doesn’t win glory, doesn’t earn credit, doesn’t make slides, doesn’t advance careers.
Genuine and Pseudo Tacit Knowledge There’s a widely-circulated saying: “A lot of what we do in this industry is tacit knowledge — AI can’t learn it.”
That’s half right, and half wrong.
Genuine tacit knowledge absolutely exists. But pseudo tacit knowledge is far more common:
Things that could have been written down, but nobody wrote them. Things that could have been structured, but the organization doesn’t reward it. Things that could have been shared, but sharing them means losing personal leverage. A huge amount of what gets called “experience” in vertical industries actually falls into the latter category. Treating pseudo tacit knowledge as genuine tacit knowledge and enshrining it has two results: first, the cost of bringing new people into the field is enormous — the only path is “follow people, watch people, put in the years”; second, AI deployment hits a wall, because what AI feeds on most is accessible, structured, verifiable context.
And the reason the software industry lets newcomers ramp up fast and AI plug in quickly is precisely that it has institutionalized the practice of making explicit what can be made explicit — if you don’t make it explicit, your PR won’t pass, CI won’t run, code review won’t approve. The process forces you to spit your knowledge out.
Behind this are two fundamentally different organizational forms.
The internet and open-source communities are organized around products, systems, collaboration, and reusability — they form a product organization.
Many traditional industries, by contrast, are expert organizations.
Dimension Product Organization Expert Organization Code / work output Code must be readable by others Whoever understands it calls the shots Interfaces / usage Interfaces must be usable by others Whoever has seniority makes the call Role of documentation Documentation must be readable by newcomers Documentation serves audits, not collaboration Problem handling Bugs must be reproducible by others Whoever stepped on the landmine first has authority Module design Modules must be replaceable Whoever controls the equipment, data, and process has the leverage Knowledge flow Knowledge must flow across teams Newcomers learn by following people, not systems Newcomer growth Depends on documentation, processes, systems, and community accumulation Depends on masters, mentors, and experience transmission Source of authority Comes from product quality, system capability, and collaboration efficiency Comes from experience, seniority, resource control, and historical accumulation An expert organization feels “safer” on the surface — because critical capability is locked inside a few people and won’t spiral out of control. But it has one fatal side effect: it is a system closed to outsiders (whether those outsiders are newcomers or agents).
Which leads to an awkward situation: AI can’t get in, because newcomers can’t get in either.
So, Where Is the Bottleneck? Back to the opening question: why can’t most industries absorb AI?
Because over the past few decades, they never built an internal system that is “friendly to outsiders.” This isn’t a problem of “AI can’t get in” — it’s a problem of “no systematic force can get in.” AI has simply shone a fresh light on it.
And the real irony is — the way many industries are investing in AI right now is still piling more “agent”-branded dashboards onto the first layer (the Tool Layer), rather than paying down the debt they’ve accumulated on the second and third layers.
The ROI Trap Most companies, when crunching the numbers on AI, are using an algorithm from a previous era. That algorithm determines that they can see at most a small sliver of AI’s value — and it also dooms their AI transformation to remain stuck at the level of “a cheaper person.”
Three Layers of ROI Here I draw on the framework from The Three Layers of ROI for AI Agents.
Layer 1 ROI: how many people AI replaced, how much time it saved, how much cost it cut.
This is the AI value that the vast majority of companies grasp most easily. Its strength is that it’s intuitive; its weakness is that it has a low ceiling.
Because at its core, it’s about making old processes cheaper and faster — it doesn’t change how the company operates. The role AI plays here is simply that of a “cheaper person.”
Layer 2 ROI: AI enables people to do things they previously couldn’t do, or couldn’t do well.
The value is far higher than Layer 1, but the barrier to entry is also far higher. It requires companies to be willing to spend on “invisible infrastructure.” The vast majority of companies get stuck at this layer. Not because they can’t do the math, but because this investment demands that the CEO have 3-5 years of patience, while most managers operate with a 6-12 month horizon.
Layer 3 ROI: the agent is part of the organization.
Its core question isn’t “what can AI do for me?”, but: what permissions does the agent hold within the organization? At this point, it is no longer just a “tool” — it becomes a new kind of actor in how the organization runs, and the company’s entire way of working must be redesigned around it.
This is what AI-Native really means. Not “we use AI,” but “the organization has redesigned how it works around AI.” The returns are non-linear, compounding, and long-term.
Why Does the Money Always Flow to Layer 1? It’s not that companies can’t see the value of Layer 2 and Layer 3 — it’s that their decision-making mechanisms can only write checks for Layer 1.
This is the most awkward part of AI transformation. The reason Layer 2 and Layer 3 ROI is so high is precisely because they are built on “paying down debt” — cleaning up dirty data, untangling broken processes, capturing scattered knowledge, and connecting isolated systems.
And this “debt” is what every manager over the past several decades racked up in pursuit of their own short-term KPIs.
So in the end, everyone settles on a kind of low-level consensus:
Build an AI system that doesn’t change power structures, doesn’t change business processes, doesn’t challenge existing KPIs — one that can be signed off on, can be demoed, can be written up in reports.
It looks like the company “uses AI,” but nothing about how the company actually operates has changed in any meaningful way.
And so, the vast majority of companies only know how to calculate Layer 1 ROI. They will naturally use AI as a cheaper person. But there’s a fatal problem here — when everyone uses AI as a cheaper person, the market will rapidly erase that value. Advantages from cost reduction are short-term, replicable, and something competitors can catch up to quickly.
The Essence of AI Transformation Isn’t Technical A somewhat uncomfortable conclusion:
The deeper AI transformation goes, the less it becomes a technical problem and the more it becomes a problem of authority, process, and organization.
The Philosophy of X-Ops The essence of turning something into Ops is transforming a complex system into a control system that can receive feedback, self-regulate, and learn.
By this point, the first three sections have laid out the problem with considerable clarity:
The software industry was hit hardest by AI, not because it understands AI best, but because it spent the last two decades building DevOps
Most traditional industries cannot absorb AI, not because AI isn’t powerful enough, but because they have no source of truth
Most enterprises’ AI transformations stall at the first layer, not because the ROI math doesn’t add up, but because their decision-making apparatus can only fund what produces visible ROI
If an industry genuinely wants to absorb AI, what it needs isn’t “AI” — it’s its own flavor of Ops. I call this X-Ops, where X can be any industry that wants to be genuinely transformed by AI.
But X-Ops is not simply “copy DevOps into another industry.” It’s a deeper way of thinking — about how an organization turns itself into a system that can be continuously observed, continuously fed back into, and continuously improved.
A Control-Theoretic Lens: The Organization as a Feedback System The core idea of control theory is remarkably simple: for any system to operate continuously in an uncertain environment and improve itself, it must have a closed loop — sense, decide, act, receive feedback, then return to sensing.
DevOps, in fact, closely resembles a control system.
When you break DevOps apart, it maps almost directly onto a control-theoretic structure:
Control Theory Concept DevOps Counterpart Controlled system Production software systems, infrastructure, user experience Target state SLO (Service Level Objective), business metrics, reliability goals, delivery targets Sensor Logs, metrics, tracing, monitoring, alerting, user feedback Controller CI/CD pipelines, release strategies, platform rules, team decisions Actuator Deployment, rollback, scaling, rate limiting, circuit breaking, feature flags Disturbance Traffic spikes, code defects, dependency failures, user behavior changes Feedback loop Monitor → assess → adjust → observe again Learning mechanism Incident postmortems, continuous improvement, automated test hardening So DevOps is not merely “automated deployment.”
The problem with the traditional development model is that feedback is too slow: requirements go live months later, incidents are only discovered after they happen, and only after ops takes over does development realize the code was never operable.
DevOps fixes this by shortening the feedback chain:
Code commit → automated testing → automated deployment → production observability → user feedback → rapid correction.
This is remarkably similar to negative feedback in control theory: detect the deviation, correct it, and bring the system back within the target range.
At the same time, “stability” in DevOps is not stillness — it is dynamic stability. This point is crucial. Traditional management likes to pursue “no change,” believing that fewer changes bring stability. The DevOps logic is closer to control theory: stability isn’t the absence of change, but remaining controllable amidst continuous change. Or, put another way:
Small-step changes, rapid detection, rapid rollback, rapid recovery, continuous learning.
Now look at the organizational level. The vast majority of companies are not a closed-loop system. They are more like an open-loop pipeline: leadership makes a call → orders are issued → execution happens → … and then nothing.
And AI’s real value is built precisely on top of closed loops. The working style that suits models best is small-step iteration: propose a change, run it, see the results, correct, and go again. If an organization itself lacks a mechanism for “runnable, verifiable, rollback-able,” then AI can’t find its place — it can’t know whether it got things right, and it can’t know where to go next.
So the first meaning of X-Ops is this: transform the organization from an open-loop system into a closed-loop system.
The Three Foundations Control theory provides an abstract lens. To make it concrete, I believe there are three foundations.
The Fact Layer
Does the system have visibility? A source of truth — this doesn’t mean all data must be piped into a data warehouse, nor that all knowledge must become a knowledge graph. But certain things must be established as “the source of fact.” You can describe it in terms of “data lineage” or “ontology,” but the core purpose is the same: solving the problem of AI-ready organizational context. This layer addresses what AI ought to trust.
The Change Record
Does the system have adjustability? AI has no hands. The physical world and the digital world are inherently separated. To prevent the gap between the two from growing ever wider, critical changes must be traceable. This can be loosely compared to Git, but the point isn’t to slap on “Git” — it’s that the act of change itself must be institutionalized. Who changed what? Why? What is the blast radius? This layer addresses whether AI can understand the organization’s evolution.
The Evaluation & Feedback System
Does the system have learnability? For the vast majority of deep learning or AI deployment projects, I’ve consistently insisted on evaluation first: build a test set → define evaluation metrics → design the evaluation methodology → evaluate → optimize → evaluate again. This approach is a universal methodology from data science, originally applied to complex system optimization problems. It generalizes far beyond its original domain.
Building Short Feedback Loops Some will say: “In our industry, safety requirements are too strict, quality demands too high, customer expectations too unforgiving — we can’t iterate fast the way the internet does.”
But the higher-risk the system, the stronger its version management should be, the more rigorous its testing, the more comprehensive its automated verification, the more meticulous its change records. In other words, the harder it is to “iterate fast,” the more an industry needs Ops — not for speed, but for control.
The essence of DevOps has never been “fast.” It’s “still controllable while being fast.” Build your own feedback cadence — it might be monthly, quarterly, annually, but it must be a closed loop.
The Human Use of Human Beings The deepest meaning of X-Ops is redefining the role of human beings within the organization.
In the past, people in organizations primarily played two roles: the executor (the one who does the work) and the decider (the one who calls the shots). The former is responsible for getting things done; the latter is responsible for deciding what to do and how. Before the age of AI, both roles were scarce — execution required skill, judgment required experience.
AI has changed this structure. Execution grows cheaper by the day, and judgment is beginning to be partially taken over by AI.
Looking back at past waves of technological change, technology rarely eliminates human value outright — it more often shifts where value resides. From the Industrial Revolution to AI, the human role has been steadily moving from “directly operating on objects” to “designing, monitoring, and regulating a system that operates on objects.”
At this point, human value hasn’t disappeared — it has moved up a level.
P vs NP offers a metaphor: finding an answer may be extraordinarily hard, but verifying one may be relatively easy. Just as a year ago we were still doing classical programming by hand, today the conversation has shifted to harness engineering — from “I build the thing myself, with my own hands” to “I define the goals, constraints, tests, acceptance criteria, and feedback loops, and let the machine generate results within a controllable range.” The critical position for humans is no longer being the producer of every specific output, but rather the higher-level evaluator, goal-setter, constraint-designer, feedback-loop builder.
Of course, people’s fear of “automation” is more a matter of control and distribution — and in the age of AI, what deepens the fear even further is that it now encroaches on the position of “evaluator” itself. But all of that is a discussion for another day.
The Three Dangers of X-Ops X-Ops sounds like a perfectly correct methodology: make things explicit, make things versioned, build feedback loops, continuously improve. But the more correct something is, the more easily it mutates inside an organization. Especially in large companies and traditional industries, any methodology eventually risks becoming new processes, new systems, new forms — even new bureaucracy.
The first danger is tool worship.
X-Ops is not “buy a set of Ops tools.” If you think bringing in Git and CI/CD means you’ve done X-Ops, you’ve most likely just wrapped a new layer of bureaucratic shell around the same old thing.
The second danger is metric alienation.
The moment you build a measurement system, someone in the organization will start optimizing the metric itself rather than the goal behind it — reward hacking. Goodhart’s Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. The goal of X-Ops is to use measurement to drive improvement, not to use measurement to punish people. The moment metrics are used to assign blame, the organization instinctively begins to fabricate data and hide problems, and you’re right back on the well-worn path to digital bureaucracy.
The third danger is the illusion of control.
The most dangerous deviation of all is treating engineering as everything — everything must be measurable, everything must be automatable, everything must be regression-testable. A truly mature X-Ops is the discipline of knowing what can be engineered, and what should not be.
Conclusion Back to the opening question.
Because the question isn’t just whether AI is there — it’s whether the industry itself has become a system that AI can understand, participate in, and optimize.
Otherwise, AI is just a more advanced shell wrapped around the same old way of operating.
Real AI transformation isn’t about putting AI into the enterprise — it’s about turning the enterprise into a system that can run together with AI.
Otherwise, so-called intelligent transformation, in the end, will merely become another form of digital bureaucracy.
summary: Why AI transformation stalls after model selection: dirty data, undocumented workflows, integration debt, evaluation gaps, and organizational resistance are the real bottlenecks.
title: The Hardest Part of AI Transformation Isn't AI — It's the Dirty Work Nobody Wants to Do

content:
If you pull apart agent memory, there are really two distinct threads running through it.
The first is personal memory: how an agent comes to understand you — your preferences, long-term context, implicit constraints, and past decisions. This kind of problem lives closer to long-context QA, and the evaluation tends to focus on “does it remember, does it understand, and can it retrieve the right user information at the right time.”
The second is procedural memory: how an agent avoids repeating mistakes, distills reusable lessons from both successes and failures, and gets better the more it runs — compounding improvement over time. This thread is concerned with things like task success rates on agentic benchmarks, tool-calling efficiency, error-rate reduction, token-cost reduction, and whether real-world usage builds genuine engineering compound interest.
This article follows the second thread.
These days, agent harnesses like Claude Code, Hermes-agent, OpenClaw, and others accumulate a wealth of trajectories over extended use. Those trajectories capture some of the most valuable signals from an agent’s interaction with its environment.
The problem, though, is that even we humans don’t make great use of these trajectories. Most of the time, they’re treated as logs, debug records, or at best stuffed back into context as retrieval fodder. An agent harness that’s been running for six months might have accumulated thousands of task trajectories — and yet the next time a similar problem comes up, the agent starts from scratch, exploring the same dead ends and stepping into the same traps.
This raises a core question:
How do you actually turn trajectories into skills?
Take a system like Hermes-agent. After enough mileage, the list of skills keeps growing. But how many of those skills are genuinely effective?
This article draws on a few recent pieces of work to discuss a question that’s becoming increasingly important: how to automatically mine, summarize, validate, and optimize agent skills from large collections of agent trajectories — skills that future tasks can actually reuse.
Hermes-agent as the Entry Point Hermes-agent has an interesting pitch: an agent that gets better the more you use it. In practice, this means distilling past experience into external memory that can be reused in the future. That external memory falls roughly into two categories.
The first is declarative memory — factual knowledge about the user, the project, the environment, and preferences. Concretely, this takes the form of USER.md and MEMORY.md. This kind of memory answers the question “how can the agent know you better.”
The second is procedural memory — skills. Concretely, this means sessions and messages written into state.db, plus the skills that get distilled into markdown files.
More specifically, the automatic skill learning in Hermes-agent is closer to a prompt-driven self-sedimentation mechanism. Its prompt gives the agent a triggering rule: when you finish a complex task, go through multiple tool calls, fix a tricky error, or discover a non-trivial workflow, you should save that approach as a skill. And if you find a skill you are using is outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately — don’t wait for the user to point it out.
SKILLS_GUIDANCE = ( "After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, " "or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a " "skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.\n" "When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, " "patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. " "Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities." ) The actual persistence is handled by the skill_manage tool, which provides a few basic actions: create creates a new SKILL.md, patch does partial replacement of an existing skill, edit does a full rewrite, write_file adds supporting files, and delete removes skills that are no longer needed.
The upside is that it is simple, direct, and interpretable. But it is also rough around the edges.
It mostly relies on the model’s own judgment to decide what experience is worth turning into a skill. It feels more like post-hoc sedimentation after a single task, rather than distilling common patterns across multiple successful and failed trajectories. There is no evaluation. Does it actually improve task success rates? Are the skills genuinely effective? Over time, the skill bank keeps growing, but which skills are still useful and which have gone stale is unclear. It demonstrates the simplest possible loop — the agent discovers a workflow while working, writes it up as a skill, and closes the circle. It also exposes the real problems that follow-up research needs to solve: how to automatically induce skills from large numbers of trajectories, how to validate skills, how to merge them, how to optimize them, and how to keep the skill bank from turning into a junkyard.
Outside the core Hermes-agent, Nous has also spun out a separate project called hermes-agent-self-evolution, which pushes this skill-sedimentation idea toward an external optimizer style of self-evolution. The idea is to read existing skills, prompts, tool descriptions, or code, combine them with execution traces, use DSPy + GEPA to generate candidate variants, and then produce a better version through evaluation, constraint checking, and human review.
# https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent-self-evolution/README.md Read current skill/prompt/tool ──► Generate eval dataset │ ▼ GEPA Optimizer ◄── Execution traces │ ▲ ▼ │ Candidate variants ──► Evaluate │ Constraint gates (tests, size limits, benchmarks) │ ▼ Best variant ──► PR against hermes-agent Think of it as an external optimizer: it treats skills / prompts / tool descriptions as optimizable objects, uses execution traces to explain why something failed, and searches for better text variants. That said, this project is still in its early stages — most features are still listed as planned.
Trace2Skill: Batch Trajectory Induction https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25158
When we’re dealing not with a single skill or a single task, but with a large collection of successful and failed trajectories, how do we automatically induce transferable skills from them?
A single trajectory from a single task is easily colored by randomness. The lesson learned from one run isn’t necessarily a stable pattern. If every local lesson gets written straight into the skill bank, you end up with fragmentation, duplication, conflicting rules, and overfitting.
Trace2Skill starts from a different premise: collect a batch of trajectories first, then induce common patterns from the overall distribution.
The full Trace2Skill pipeline breaks down into three stages:
Stage 1: Trajectory Generation frozen agent + initial skill S0 → roll out on evolution tasks → collect successful trajectories T+ and failed trajectories T- Stage 2: Parallel Multi-Agent Patch Proposal dispatch one analyst sub-agent per trajectory → success analyzer A+ summarizes winning patterns → failure analyzer A- performs agentic root-cause analysis → each trajectory produces a trajectory-local skill patch Stage 3: Conflict-Free Patch Consolidation merge all patches hierarchically → deduplicate, resolve conflicts, filter out spurious patterns → distill into a unified skill directory S* In essence, this decomposes “from trajectories to skill” into three sub-problems:
Where does experience come from? From the agent’s real rollouts in the task environment. How is local experience expressed? As trajectory-local patches, not written directly into the final skill. How do multiple local experiences become a stable skill? Through hierarchical merging, conflict detection, and format validation — culminating in a unified skill directory. That said, Trace2Skill isn’t a full-blown self-learning agent operating in a real environment. In the paper and codebase, it’s evaluated on SpreadsheetBench, where the error analyst can compare against ground truth and verify a failure cause with minimal repair. This works well for benchmarking.
But in real-world settings, most tasks don’t have a clear ground truth. Whether an analysis report is good enough, or whether a code change matches the user’s intent — these are hard to judge with a single evaluator.
SkillOpt: Treating Skills as Trainable External Parameters https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23904
Can a skill be further optimized? In other words, is a skill just a one-shot summary of experience, or can it be continuously trained through feedback — like model parameters? This is precisely where SkillOpt enters the picture.
SkillOpt asks: given a current skill, how well does it perform across a batch of tasks? Which rules should be added, deleted, or replaced? Did the change actually make the agent stronger? And if it didn’t, should the change be rejected?
The implication is that skills aren’t just experience-summary documents — they’re external state that can be optimized. SkillOpt borrows ideas from deep learning and applies them to the optimization of natural-language skills. The basic setup: the target model stays frozen; the execution harness stays frozen; the task environment stays frozen. The only thing being optimized is the external skill document — through text-space optimization, analogous to deep learning training.
The SkillOpt pipeline breaks down into several steps:
current skill S_t ↓ target agent runs a batch of tasks with S_t → scored trajectories ↓ optimizer model analyzes success/failure mini-batches ↓ proposes add / delete / replace edits ↓ caps each round's edits via a textual learning rate ↓ validates on a held-out selection split ↓ accept only if score improves; otherwise reject and log as negative feedback ↓ after multiple epochs, export best_skill.md The optimizer model can propose changes, but whether they’re accepted isn’t determined by the model’s confidence — it’s determined by the held-out validation score.
How It Works Forward pass: the target agent runs a rollout batch
The frozen target agent takes the current skill and executes a batch of tasks. The harness records task metadata, messages, tool calls, observations, command outputs, final answers, verifier feedback, and benchmark-specific context.
This mirrors the forward pass in deep learning — except the output isn’t a loss tensor. It’s:
scored trajectories: task current skill agent messages tool calls observations final answer verifier feedback score This is critical: SkillOpt depends on scored rollouts. Without a score, the downstream validation gate has nothing to work with.
Backward pass: minibatch reflection
These trajectories tell the optimizer two kinds of things:
Successful trajectories: which rules in the current skill are likely working, and which behaviors should be preserved. Failed trajectories: what guidance the current skill is missing, which rules aren’t precise enough, and which actions need to be prohibited or replaced. SkillOpt doesn’t cram all trajectories into the optimizer model at once. Instead, it splits successes and failures into reflection mini-batches.
Failure mini-batches produce corrective edits. They answer:
What common problems do these failed trajectories expose? What checking steps is the current skill missing? Which existing rules are too vague? Which behaviors should be explicitly banned? Success mini-batches produce preservation edits. They answer:
Which behaviors led to success? Which rules should be kept or emphasized? Which effective workflows shouldn't be broken by future edits? Add / Delete / Replace
The optimizer model’s output isn’t a full rewrite of the skill — it’s a set of structured edits. Local edits make each update more controllable and auditable, as opposed to rewriting the entire SKILL.md.
Textual Learning Rate
Skill updates are constrained as controlled textual operations. In model training there’s a learning rate; SkillOpt brings this idea to text-space: each round limits how many edits can be applied. This addresses the common problem where agent skills easily “learn into chaos.” The core insight: skill evolution should be continuous, not a tear-down-and-rebuild every time.
Validation Gate
After each round produces a candidate skill, SkillOpt has the same frozen target agent re-run tasks on the held-out selection split using that candidate. The modification is only accepted if the candidate skill’s score strictly exceeds the current skill’s score.
Rejected-Edit Buffer
If a candidate skill doesn’t pass the validation gate, SkillOpt doesn’t simply discard the attempt. It records the rejected edits, telling future optimizer runs:
Which modification directions were tried but failed; Which rules seemed reasonable but hurt performance; Which failure patterns still haven't been resolved; Which edits caused regressions. It’s essentially an optimizer memory, but more granular: which skill edits were attempted but failed validation.
Final output: best_skill.md
Limitations SkillOpt has clear limitations.
First, it’s heavily dependent on scored trajectories. Tasks need a reasonably reliable scoring mechanism — whether exact match, unit tests, a benchmark evaluator, or LLM-as-judge. You need some way to produce comparable scores. This is relatively straightforward in benchmarks, but much harder in real-world environments.
AutoSkill: Online Lifelong Skill Lifecycle https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01145
How should skills be continuously generated, maintained, and reused?
As users interact with an agent over the long term, they keep expressing stable preferences, work habits, formatting requirements, task constraints, and operational patterns. AutoSkill attempts to turn these transient interaction experiences into explicit, reusable, maintainable, and retrievable skills.
Real user-agent interactions are very different from benchmark trajectories.
In a benchmark, task boundaries are clear, inputs and outputs are well-defined, and there’s often an evaluator. Whether a spreadsheet task is solved correctly, whether code passes tests, whether a web task is completed — these can all be scored fairly directly.
Real user interactions are rarely like that. Users repeatedly express needs that aren’t really “task-shaped.” These don’t have a single right answer like an exam question, but they matter a lot.
Traditional memory can preserve these preferences, but memory tends to be just facts or fragments. For example:
The user prefers a formal style. The user dislikes excessive technical jargon. The project uses pnpm. This kind of memory handles “knowing what,” but doesn’t necessarily translate into “how to do it later.” AutoSkill’s goal is to further abstract these recurring interaction experiences into skills — procedural artifacts with trigger conditions, behavioral goals, constraints, and execution methods.
So AutoSkill’s core insight is this: user interaction experience shouldn’t just be saved as memory — it should be abstracted into executable, long-lived skills.
The overall closed loop:
User conversation / agent trajectory / conversation history ↓ Extract reusable skill candidate ↓ Retrieve similar existing skills ↓ Decision: discard / add / merge ↓ Write or update SKILL.md in SkillBank ↓ On future requests: query rewrite + hybrid retrieval ↓ Inject relevant skills into LLM prompt ↓ New feedback triggers another round of skill evolution At the heart of it are two loops.
The first is the response generation loop. When a new request arrives, AutoSkill rewrites the retrieval query based on the current query and historical context, retrieves relevant skills from the SkillBank, injects them into the prompt, and helps the model generate a response.
The second is the skill evolution loop. After each interaction, the system tries to extract reusable skill candidates from user input and interaction traces, then decides whether the candidate should be discarded, added as a new skill, or merged into an existing skill.
The key design point in AutoSkill: new candidates don’t go straight into the SkillBank. They first retrieve the most similar existing skills, and only the candidate plus a small set of nearest-neighbor skills are sent to the management judge — not the entire SkillBank jammed into the prompt. This serves two purposes: first, it avoids skill duplication; second, it keeps the management decision scalable.
The management judge makes three types of decisions:
discard: The candidate is too vague, low-signal, non-transferable, one-off, or already covered by the existing bank. add: The candidate represents a new durable capability. merge: The candidate and an existing skill belong to the same capability; the existing skill should be updated. The decision is based on four dimensions:
job-to-be-done deliverable type hard constraints / success criteria required tools / workflow Only merge when stripping away instance-level details still leaves the same capability; otherwise add; if it’s not worth keeping long-term, discard.
If the decision is merge, AutoSkill invokes the skill merging module. This isn’t simple text concatenation — it performs a semantic union:
Preserve the original skill's capability identity. Incorporate the candidate's new reusable constraints, examples, and execution details. Remove duplicate content. Drop outdated or irrelevant topic constraints. Avoid regression — keep the important checks from the original skill. Don't introduce new standards the user never explicitly set. After merging, the skill version number increments.
AutoSkill persists skills as regular files. The default layout:
SkillBank/ Users/<user_id>/<skill-slug>/SKILL.md Common/<skill-slug>/SKILL.md vectors/ <embedding-signature>.meta.json <embedding-signature>.ids.txt <embedding-signature>.vecs.f32 index/ skills-bm25.* skill_usage_stats.json Users/<user_id> holds user-personalized skills, Common holds shared skills, vectors caches embeddings, and index stores the BM25 index and usage statistics.
That said, AutoSkill has limitations.
The main pipeline relies heavily on an LLM to judge whether a candidate is worth saving, whether it should be merged, and how to merge — rather than being based on scored validation.
